info.text: In this example we'll explore the JavaScript Date object be remixing a classic piece of conceptual art, the Date Paintings by On Kawara.
info.title: getting started
Date object
info.text: We've defined a single function called onKawara. In the center of our function we've created a variable d which is a new instance of the JavaScript Date object, this snapshots a specific moment in time and provides methods for displaying different aspects of this moment.

info.title: day of the month
info.text: For example, the .getDate() method gives us the number value of the current day of the month. An important piece of data we'll need to recreate On Kawara's Date Painting in the browser.

info.title: current month index
info.text: Another important piece of data we needs is the current month. The .getMonth() gives us the number value of the current month (from 0 - 11), which we'll need to translate into the month's name.

info.title: months array
info.text: To do this, we'll create an array with all the abbreviated month names, the way On Kawara would often write them in his paintings.

info.title: month name
info.text: Then we'll create a new variable month which will grab the month name matching the index value of the current month we got from the Date object.

info.title: year
info.text: The last bit of data we need from the Date object is the year which we can get with the .getFullYear() method.

info.title: date string
info.text: Now we can construct a string using JavaScript's template literals syntax so that we can not only combine these three pieces of data, but also add the . and space after the month, as well as the , and space after before the year.

info.title: DOM API
info.text: We've constructed a string with the current date, next we need to use the Web's DOM API to modify our page using the document object. We first use this object at the top of our function to change the page's background color to 'black'. It's worth noting that even though we haven't explicitly defined a <body> element in our HTML, the browser will automatically create one, which is why we can still modify it here.

info.title: body CSS
info.text: Next we ensure the body's height matches that of our viewport (the entire window) and set it's display property to 'grid' in order to use the CSS Grid properties.

info.title: CSS Grid
info.text: When an element has a single child, CSS Grid makes it really easy to center that child within it both vertically using the alignItems property, and horizontally with the the justifyContent property.

info.title: create the span
info.text: The body doesn't have any elements in it to center by default, which is why we create one at the end of our function using the DOM API's createElement() method. Here we've used it to create a new <span> element.

info.title: text content
info.text: Next we set the new span element's content to the dateString we created.

info.title: span CSS
info.text: We can also modify the element's CSS as we did with the body. I've defined a font-stack which prioritizes Futura because On Kawara would often use this typeface.

info.title: append span
info.text: Lastly, we append this new element to our body so that it gets rendered to the page.

info.title: on load
info.text: As with many of our other demos, the last line in our code sets everything going by calling our function as soon as our page has loaded.

info.title: lots more
info.text: There's lots more we could do with the Date object, including access the current time, down to the millisecond. Rendering this data is a pretty straight forward use, but we could also use this to create conditional logic (maybe a page is only accessible at a certain time) or to create an animation (maybe to control a sunrise that is synced to real life).

info.title: no libraries
info.text: On Kawara would create each date painting by hand from scratch. In a way I've done the same, having hand coded it from "scratch" without the use of any JavaScript libraries. That said, nothing is every really coded from scratch, as explained in the APIs + Libraries JS Doc. We've kept this demo "vanilla" but we'll be making use of many different creative coding libraries through out the other lessons.
